To address these gaps, we introduce \textbf{SLIP} (\textbf{S}ensor \textbf{L}anguage-\textbf{I}nformed \textbf{P}retraining), an open-source framework for learning language-aligned representations that generalize across diverse sensor setups. SLIP integrates contrastive alignment with sensor-conditioned captioning, facilitating both discriminative understanding and generative reasoning. By repurposing a pretrained decoder-only language model via cross-attention and introducing an elegant, flexible patch-embedder, SLIP supports different temporal resolutions and variable-length input at inference time without additional retraining. Across 11 datasets, SLIP demonstrates superior performance in zero-shot transfer, signal captioning, and question answering. It achieves a 77.14% average linear-probing accuracy, a 5.93% relative improvement over strong baselines, and reaches 64.83% accuracy in sensor-based question answering.

AI.tldr: SLIP is a flexible sensor-language alignment framework that uses contrastive alignment and captioning to learn transferable representations across diverse sensor setups without requiring retraining for different configurations.
AI.motivation: Modern sensing systems generate large volumes of unlabeled multivariate time-series data, but most SSL approaches optimized for reconstruction/forecasting fail to capture semantic structure needed for downstream tasks. Existing sensor-language alignment methods are limited to fixed sensor configurations (predefined channels, lengths, resolutions), hindering cross-domain applicability.
AI.method: SLIP integrates contrastive alignment with sensor-conditioned captioning. It repurposes a pretrained decoder-only language model via cross-attention and introduces a flexible patch-embedder that supports different temporal resolutions and variable-length input at inference without retraining.
AI.result: Across 11 datasets, SLIP demonstrates superior performance in zero-shot transfer, signal captioning, and question answering. Achieves 77.14% average linear-probing accuracy (5.93% relative improvement over baselines) and 64.83% accuracy in sensor-based question answering.
AI.conclusion: SLIP provides an open-source framework for learning language-aligned representations that generalize across diverse sensor setups, addressing limitations of fixed-configuration approaches while enabling both discriminative understanding and generative reasoning.

title: CAETC: Causal Autoencoding and Treatment Conditioning for Counterfactual Estimation over Time
summary: Counterfactual estimation over time is important in various applications, such as personalized medicine. However, time-dependent confounding bias in observational data still poses a significant challenge in achieving accurate and efficient estimation. We introduce causal autoencoding and treatment conditioning (CAETC), a novel method for this problem. Built on adversarial representation learning, our method leverages an autoencoding architecture to learn a partially invertible and treatment-invariant representation, where the outcome prediction task is cast as applying a treatment-specific conditioning on the representation. Our design is independent of the underlying sequence model and can be applied to existing architectures such as long short-term memories (LSTMs) or temporal convolution networks (TCNs). We conduct extensive experiments on synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world data to demonstrate that CAETC yields significant improvement in counterfactual estimation over existing methods.

AI.tldr: CAETC is a novel method for counterfactual estimation over time that uses causal autoencoding with treatment conditioning to handle time-dependent confounding bias in observational data.
AI.motivation: Time-dependent confounding bias in observational data poses significant challenges for accurate counterfactual estimation in applications like personalized medicine.
AI.method: CAETC uses adversarial representation learning with an autoencoding architecture to learn partially invertible and treatment-invariant representations, then applies treatment-specific conditioning for outcome prediction. The method is independent of underlying sequence models and can be applied to architectures like LSTMs or TCNs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world data demonstrate that CAETC yields significant improvement in counterfactual estimation over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: CAETC provides an effective solution for counterfactual estimation over time by addressing time-dependent confounding bias through causal autoencoding and treatment conditioning.

title: Normative Common Ground Replication (NormCoRe): Replication-by-Translation for Studying Norms in Multi-agent AI
comment: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (ACM FAccT '26)
summary: In the late 2010s, the fashion trend NormCore framed sameness as a signal of belonging, illustrating how norms emerge through collective coordination. Today, similar forms of normative coordination can be observed in systems based on Multi-agent Artificial Intelligence (MAAI), as AI-based agents deliberate, negotiate, and converge on shared decisions in fairness-sensitive domains. Yet, existing empirical approaches often treat norms as targets for alignment or replication, implicitly assuming equivalence between human subjects and AI agents and leaving collective normative dynamics insufficiently examined. To address this gap, we propose Normative Common Ground Replication (NormCoRe), a novel methodological framework to systematically translate the design of human subject experiments into MAAI environments. Building on behavioral science, replication research, and state-of-the-art MAAI architectures, NormCoRe maps the structural layers of human subject studies onto the design of AI agent studies, enabling systematic documentation of study design and analysis of norms in MAAI. We demonstrate the utility of NormCoRe by replicating a seminal experimental study on distributive justice, in which participants negotiate fairness principles under a "veil of ignorance". We show that normative judgments in AI agent studies can differ from human baselines and are sensitive to the choice of the foundation model and the language used to instantiate agent personas. Our work provides a principled pathway for analyzing norms in MAAI and helps to guide, reflect, and document design choices whenever AI agents are used to automate or support tasks formerly carried out by humans.

AI.tldr: A framework called NormCoRe translates human subject experiments into multi-agent AI environments to study normative dynamics, showing AI agents' normative judgments differ from humans and are sensitive to model choices.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches treat norms as targets for alignment or replication, assuming equivalence between humans and AI agents, leaving collective normative dynamics in multi-agent AI systems insufficiently examined.
AI.method: Propose Normative Common Ground Replication (NormCoRe), a methodological framework that systematically maps structural layers of human subject experiments onto AI agent study designs, enabling documentation and analysis of norms in multi-agent AI.
AI.result: Normative judgments in AI agent studies differ from human baselines and are sensitive to the choice of foundation model and language used to instantiate agent personas, as demonstrated by replicating a distributive justice experiment.
AI.conclusion: NormCoRe provides a principled pathway for analyzing norms in multi-agent AI and helps guide, reflect, and document design choices when AI agents automate or support tasks formerly done by humans.

title: Survival Meets Classification: A Novel Framework for Early Risk Prediction Models of Chronic Diseases
summary: Chronic diseases are long-lasting conditions that require lifelong medical attention. Using big EMR data, we have developed early disease risk prediction models for five common chronic diseases: diabetes, hypertension, CKD, COPD, and chronic ischemic heart disease. In this study, we present a novel approach for disease risk models by integrating survival analysis with classification techniques. Traditional models for predicting the risk of chronic diseases predominantly focus on either survival analysis or classification independently. In this paper, we show survival analysis methods can be re-engineered to enable them to do classification efficiently and effectively, thereby making them a comprehensive tool for developing disease risk surveillance models. The results of our experiments on real-world big EMR data show that the performance of survival models in terms of accuracy, F1 score, and AUROC is comparable to or better than that of prior state-of-the-art models like LightGBM and XGBoost. Lastly, the proposed survival models use a novel methodology to generate explanations, which have been clinically validated by a panel of three expert physicians.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed chronic disease risk prediction models by integrating survival analysis with classification techniques, achieving performance comparable to or better than state-of-the-art models like LightGBM and XGBoost on big EMR data.
AI.motivation: Chronic diseases require lifelong medical attention, and traditional risk prediction models for these diseases typically use either survival analysis or classification independently, lacking a comprehensive approach.
AI.method: A novel approach that re-engineers survival analysis methods to perform classification efficiently and effectively, integrating survival analysis with classification techniques for disease risk surveillance models.
AI.result: The proposed survival models performed comparably or better than prior state-of-the-art models (LightGBM and XGBoost) in accuracy, F1 score, and AUROC on real-world big EMR data for five chronic diseases (diabetes, hypertension, CKD, COPD, and chronic ischemic heart disease).
AI.conclusion: Survival analysis methods can be successfully adapted for classification tasks, creating comprehensive disease risk surveillance models with clinically validated explanations, offering a promising approach for chronic disease risk prediction.

title: LABSHIELD: A Multimodal Benchmark for Safety-Critical Reasoning and Planning in Scientific Laboratories
summary: Artificial intelligence is increasingly catalyzing scientific automation, with multimodal large language model (MLLM) agents evolving from lab assistants into self-driving lab operators. This transition imposes stringent safety requirements on laboratory environments, where fragile glassware, hazardous substances, and high-precision laboratory equipment render planning errors or misinterpreted risks potentially irreversible. However, the safety awareness and decision-making reliability of embodied agents in such high-stakes settings remain insufficiently defined and evaluated. To bridge this gap, we introduce LABSHIELD, a realistic multi-view benchmark designed to assess MLLMs in hazard identification and safety-critical reasoning. Grounded in U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) standards and the Globally Harmonized System (GHS), LABSHIELD establishes a rigorous safety taxonomy spanning 164 operational tasks with diverse manipulation complexities and risk profiles. We evaluate 20 proprietary models, 9 open-source models, and 3 embodied models under a dual-track evaluation framework. Our results reveal a systematic gap between general-domain MCQ accuracy and Semi-open QA safety performance, with models exhibiting an average drop of 32.0% in professional laboratory scenarios, particularly in hazard interpretation and safety-aware planning. These findings underscore the urgent necessity for safety-centric reasoning frameworks to ensure reliable autonomous scientific experimentation in embodied laboratory contexts. The full dataset will be released soon.

AI.tldr: LABSHIELD benchmark tests MLLM agents' safety awareness in laboratory automation, revealing significant performance gaps in hazard identification.
AI.motivation: As AI automates scientific labs, safety requirements become critical due to fragile glassware, hazardous substances, and precision equipment. Current MLLM agents lack adequate safety awareness and decision-making reliability for these high-risk environments.
AI.method: Develop LABSHIELD benchmark based on OSHA and GHS standards with 164 operational tasks covering diverse manipulation complexities and risk profiles. Evaluate 20 proprietary, 9 open-source, and 3 embodied models using dual-track evaluation framework comparing MCQ accuracy vs Semi-open QA safety performance.
AI.result: Models show average 32.0% performance drop in professional laboratory scenarios, especially in hazard interpretation and safety-aware planning. Reveals systematic gap between general-domain accuracy and safety-critical reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: Urgent need for safety-centric reasoning frameworks to ensure reliable autonomous scientific experimentation in embodied laboratory contexts. Dataset will be publicly released.

title: Hybrid Energy-Aware Reward Shaping: A Unified Lightweight Physics-Guided Methodology for Policy Optimization
summary: Deep reinforcement learning excels in continuous control but often requires extensive exploration, while physics-based models demand complete equations and suffer cubic complexity. This study proposes Hybrid Energy-Aware Reward Shaping (H-EARS), unifying potential-based reward shaping with energy-aware action regularization. H-EARS constrains action magnitude while balancing task-specific and energy-based potentials via functional decomposition, achieving linear complexity O(n) by capturing dominant energy components without full dynamics. We establish a theoretical foundation including: (1) functional independence for separate task/energy optimization; (2) energy-based convergence acceleration; (3) convergence guarantees under function approximation; and (4) approximate potential error bounds. Lyapunov stability connections are analyzed as heuristic guides. Experiments across baselines show improved convergence, stability, and energy efficiency. Vehicle simulations validate applicability in safety-critical domains under extreme conditions. Results confirm that integrating lightweight physics priors enhances model-free RL without complete system models, enabling transfer from lab research to industrial applications.

AI.tldr: H-EARS combines physics priors with model-free RL using energy-aware reward shaping, achieving linear complexity and improved efficiency without full system models.
AI.motivation: Deep RL requires extensive exploration while physics-based models need complete equations with high complexity. Need a method that integrates lightweight physics priors into model-free RL without requiring full system dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes Hybrid Energy-Aware Reward Shaping (H-EARS), unifying potential-based reward shaping with energy-aware action regularization. Uses functional decomposition to balance task-specific and energy-based potentials, achieving O(n) complexity by capturing dominant energy components.
AI.result: Experiments show improved convergence, stability, and energy efficiency across baselines. Vehicle simulations validate applicability in safety-critical domains under extreme conditions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating lightweight physics priors enhances model-free RL without complete system models, enabling practical transfer from lab research to industrial applications.

title: Few-for-Many Personalized Federated Learning
summary: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) aims to train customized models for clients with highly heterogeneous data distributions while preserving data privacy. Existing approaches often rely on heuristics like clustering or model interpolation, which lack principled mechanisms for balancing heterogeneous client objectives. Serving $M$ clients with distinct data distributions is inherently a multi-objective optimization problem, where achieving optimal personalization ideally requires $M$ distinct models on the Pareto front. However, maintaining $M$ separate models poses significant scalability challenges in federated settings with hundreds or thousands of clients. To address this challenge, we reformulate PFL as a few-for-many optimization problem that maintains only $K$ shared server models ($K \ll M$) to collectively serve all $M$ clients. We prove that this framework achieves near-optimal personalization: the approximation error diminishes as $K$ increases and each client's model converges to each client's optimum as data grows. Building on this reformulation, we propose FedFew, a practical algorithm that jointly optimizes the $K$ server models through efficient gradient-based updates. Unlike clustering-based approaches that require manual client partitioning or interpolation-based methods that demand careful hyperparameter tuning, FedFew automatically discovers the optimal model diversity through its optimization process. Experiments across vision, NLP, and real-world medical imaging datasets demonstrate that FedFew, with just 3 models, consistently outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/pgg3/FedFew.

AI.tldr: FedFew reformulates personalized federated learning as a few-for-many optimization problem, maintaining only K shared server models to serve M clients efficiently while achieving near-optimal personalization.
AI.motivation: Existing personalized federated learning approaches rely on heuristics like clustering or model interpolation, lacking principled mechanisms for balancing heterogeneous client objectives. Maintaining M separate models for M clients is impractical due to scalability challenges in federated settings with hundreds or thousands of clients.
AI.method: FedFew reformulates PFL as a few-for-many optimization problem, maintaining only K shared server models (K << M) to collectively serve all M clients. The framework uses gradient-based updates to jointly optimize the K server models, automatically discovering optimal model diversity through optimization rather than manual partitioning or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: FedFew demonstrates near-optimal personalization: approximation error diminishes as K increases, and each client's model converges to its optimum as data grows. Experiments across vision, NLP, and real-world medical imaging datasets show that FedFew with just 3 models consistently outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: FedFew provides a principled and scalable solution to personalized federated learning by reformulating it as a few-for-many optimization problem, efficiently serving many clients with few shared models while maintaining near-optimal performance without requiring manual client partitioning or extensive hyperparameter tuning.

title: AutoScout: Structured Optimization for Automating ML System Configuration
summary: Machine learning (ML) systems expose a rapidly expanding configuration space spanning model-parallelism strategies, communication optimizations, and low-level runtime parameters. End-to-end system efficiency is highly sensitive to these choices, yet identifying high-performance configurations is challenging due to heterogeneous feature types (e.g., sparse and dense parameters), conditional dependencies (e.g., valid execution parameters only under specific upstream decisions), and the high search (profiling) cost. Existing approaches either optimize a narrow subset of configuration dimensions or rely on ad-hoc heuristics that fail to generalize as configuration spaces continue to grow. We present AutoScout, a general-purpose systems configurator for ML training, fine-tuning, and inference. It formulates the system configuration as a mixed-discrete/continuous optimization problem with hierarchical dependencies and introduces a hybrid optimization framework that jointly refines sparse structural decisions and dense execution parameters. To reduce profiling cost, AutoScout adaptively prioritizes high-impact configuration features and ensembles simulators with varying fidelity. Across diverse models, hardware platforms, and deployment objectives, AutoScout consistently identifies high-performance configurations, achieving 2.7-3.0$\times$ training speedup over expert-tuned settings.

AI.tldr: AutoScout: A hybrid optimization framework for automatically finding high-performance ML system configurations across training, fine-tuning, and inference, achieving 2.7-3.0× speedup over expert-tuned settings.
AI.motivation: ML systems have complex configuration spaces with heterogeneous feature types, conditional dependencies, and high profiling costs. Existing approaches either optimize narrow subsets or use ad-hoc heuristics that don't scale with growing configuration spaces.
AI.method: Formulates ML system configuration as a mixed-discrete/continuous optimization problem with hierarchical dependencies. Uses hybrid optimization framework that jointly refines sparse structural decisions and dense execution parameters. Reduces profiling cost through adaptive feature prioritization and ensemble simulators with varying fidelity.
AI.result: AutoScout consistently identifies high-performance configurations across diverse models, hardware platforms, and deployment objectives, achieving 2.7-3.0× training speedup over expert-tuned settings.
AI.conclusion: AutoScout provides a general-purpose solution for ML system configuration that effectively handles complex, growing configuration spaces while reducing profiling costs, significantly outperforming manual expert tuning.

title: Can RL Improve Generalization of LLM Agents? An Empirical Study
summary: Reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) has shown promise for training LLM agents to perform multi-turn decision-making based on environment feedback. However, most existing evaluations remain largely in-domain: training and testing are conducted in the same environment or even on the same tasks. In real-world deployment, agents may operate in unseen environments with different background knowledge, observation spaces, and action interfaces. To characterize the generalization profile of RFT under such shifts, we conduct a systematic study along three axes: (1) within-environment generalization across task difficulty, (2) cross-environment transfer to unseen environments, and (3) sequential multi-environment training to quantify transfer and forgetting. Our results show that RFT generalizes well across task difficulty within an environment, but exhibits weaker transfer to unseen environments, which correlates with shifts in both semantic priors and observation/action interfaces. In contrast, sequential training yields promising downstream gains with minimal upstream forgetting, and mixture training across environments improves the overall balance. We further provide detailed analyses and deeper insights, and hope our work helps the community develop and deploy generalizable LLM agents.

AI.tldr: RFT shows strong within-environment generalization across task difficulty but weak cross-environment transfer; sequential and mixture training improve generalization with minimal forgetting.
AI.motivation: Current RFT evaluations are largely in-domain, but real-world deployment requires agents to operate in unseen environments with different background knowledge, observation spaces, and action interfaces.
AI.method: Systematic study along three axes: (1) within-environment generalization across task difficulty, (2) cross-environment transfer to unseen environments, and (3) sequential multi-environment training to quantify transfer and forgetting.
AI.result: RFT generalizes well across task difficulty within an environment, but exhibits weaker transfer to unseen environments, correlating with shifts in semantic priors and observation/action interfaces. Sequential training yields promising downstream gains with minimal upstream forgetting, and mixture training improves overall balance.
AI.conclusion: The study provides detailed analyses and insights to help develop and deploy generalizable LLM agents, highlighting the need for methods that improve cross-environment transfer while maintaining performance.

title: Fractional Rotation, Full Potential? Investigating Performance and Convergence of Partial RoPE
summary: Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) is a common choice in transformer architectures for encoding relative positional information. Although earlier work has examined omitting RoPE in specific layers, the effect of varying the fraction of hidden dimensions that receive rotary transformations remains largely unexplored. This design choice can yield substantial memory savings, which becomes especially significant at long context lengths. We find up to 10x memory savings over the standard RoPE cache, while achieving comparable final loss. In this work, we present a systematic study examining the impact of partial RoPE on training dynamics and convergence across architectures and datasets. Our findings uncover several notable patterns: (1) applying RoPE to only a small fraction of dimensions (around 10%) achieves convergence comparable to using full RoPE; (2) these trends hold consistently across model size, sequence lengths and datasets of varying quality and architectures, with higher-quality data resulting in lower overall loss and similar benchmark performance; and (3) some models trained with NoPE (No Positional Encoding) showcase unstable learning trajectories, which can be alleviated through minimal RoPE application or QK-Norm which converges to a higher loss. Together, these results offer practical guidance for model designers aiming to balance efficiency and training stability, while emphasizing the previously overlooked importance of partial RoPE.

AI.tldr: Using only 10% of dimensions for Rotary Positional Embedding achieves comparable performance to full RoPE while providing up to 10x memory savings, especially beneficial for long-context models.
AI.motivation: The effect of varying the fraction of hidden dimensions that receive rotary transformations is largely unexplored, despite potential for substantial memory savings at long context lengths.
AI.method: Systematic study examining the impact of partial RoPE on training dynamics and convergence across different architectures and datasets.
AI.result: Applying RoPE to only ~10% of dimensions achieves convergence comparable to full RoPE; trends hold across model sizes, sequence lengths, and datasets; NoPE models show unstable learning that can be alleviated with minimal RoPE or QK-Norm.
AI.conclusion: Partial RoPE offers practical efficiency-stability balance for model designers, emphasizing the previously overlooked importance of this design choice for memory savings without performance degradation.

title: XSkill: Continual Learning from Experience and Skills in Multimodal Agents
summary: Multimodal agents can now tackle complex reasoning tasks with diverse tools, yet they still suffer from inefficient tool use and inflexible orchestration in open-ended settings. A central challenge is enabling such agents to continually improve without parameter updates by learning from past trajectories. We identify two complementary forms of reusable knowledge essential for this goal: experiences, providing concise action-level guidance for tool selection and decision making, and skills, providing structured task-level guidance for planning and tool use. To this end, we propose XSkill, a dual-stream framework for continual learning from experience and skills in multimodal agents. XSkill grounds both knowledge extraction and retrieval in visual observations. During accumulation, XSkill distills and consolidates experiences and skills from multi-path rollouts via visually grounded summarization and cross-rollout critique. During inference, it retrieves and adapts this knowledge to the current visual context and feeds usage history back into accumulation to form a continual learning loop. Evaluated on five benchmarks across diverse domains with four backbone models, XSkill consistently and substantially outperforms both tool-only and learning-based baselines. Further analysis reveals that the two knowledge streams play complementary roles in influencing the reasoning behaviors of agents and show superior zero-shot generalization.

AI.tldr: XSkill is a dual-stream framework that enables multimodal agents to continually learn from experiences and skills without parameter updates, achieving superior performance and zero-shot generalization across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: Multimodal agents currently suffer from inefficient tool use and inflexible orchestration in open-ended settings, and lack the ability to continually improve without parameter updates by learning from past trajectories.
AI.method: XSkill is a dual-stream framework for continual learning from experience and skills in multimodal agents. It grounds both knowledge extraction and retrieval in visual observations. During accumulation, it distills and consolidates experiences and skills from multi-path rollouts via visually grounded summarization and cross-rollout critique. During inference, it retrieves and adapts this knowledge to the current visual context and feeds usage history back into accumulation to form a continual learning loop.
AI.result: XSkill consistently and substantially outperforms both tool-only and learning-based baselines across five benchmarks in diverse domains with four backbone models. The two knowledge streams (experiences and skills) play complementary roles in influencing agents' reasoning behaviors and show superior zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: The XSkill framework successfully enables multimodal agents to continually improve without parameter updates by learning from past trajectories through complementary experience and skill knowledge streams, demonstrating significant performance improvements and superior generalization capabilities.

title: Personalized Federated Learning via Gaussian Generative Modeling
summary: Federated learning has emerged as a paradigm to train models collaboratively on inherently distributed client data while safeguarding privacy. In this context, personalized federated learning tackles the challenge of data heterogeneity by equipping each client with a dedicated model. A prevalent strategy decouples the model into a shared feature extractor and a personalized classifier head, where the latter actively guides the representation learning. However, previous works have focused on classifier head-guided personalization, neglecting the potential personalized characteristics in the representation distribution. Building on this insight, we propose pFedGM, a method based on Gaussian generative modeling. The approach begins by training a Gaussian generator that models client heterogeneity via weighted re-sampling. A balance between global collaboration and personalization is then struck by employing a dual objective: a shared objective that maximizes inter-class distance across clients, and a local objective that minimizes intra-class distance within them. To achieve this, we decouple the conventional Gaussian classifier into a navigator for global optimization, and a statistic extractor for capturing distributional statistics. Inspired by the Kalman gain, the algorithm then employs a dual-scale fusion framework at global and local levels to equip each client with a personalized classifier head. In this framework, we model the global representation distribution as a prior and the client-specific data as the likelihood, enabling Bayesian inference for class probability estimation. The evaluation covers a comprehensive range of scenarios: heterogeneity in class counts, environmental corruption, and multiple benchmark datasets and configurations. pFedGM achieves superior or competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: pFedGM introduces Gaussian generative modeling for personalized federated learning, addressing data heterogeneity through dual objectives (global inter-class distance maximization and local intra-class distance minimization) and a dual-scale fusion framework inspired by Kalman gain.
AI.motivation: Previous personalized federated learning methods focus on classifier head-guided personalization but neglect personalized characteristics in representation distribution. The paper aims to address this gap by modeling client heterogeneity more comprehensively.
AI.method: pFedGM uses Gaussian generative modeling with weighted re-sampling to capture client heterogeneity. It decouples the Gaussian classifier into a navigator for global optimization and statistic extractor for distribution statistics. A dual-scale fusion framework combines global (prior) and local (likelihood) information for Bayesian inference.
AI.result: pFedGM achieves superior or competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods across various scenarios including heterogeneity in class counts, environmental corruption, and multiple benchmark datasets/configurations.
AI.conclusion: pFedGM successfully addresses representation distribution heterogeneity in personalized federated learning through Gaussian generative modeling and dual-scale fusion, outperforming existing methods in diverse heterogeneous scenarios.

title: A Robust and Efficient Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for Traffic Signal Control
comment: 12 pages, 4 tables, 8 figures. Under review in the 31st ITS World Congress 2026
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) in Traffic Signal Control (TSC) faces significant hurdles in real-world deployment due to limited generalization to dynamic traffic flow variations. Existing approaches often overfit static patterns and use action spaces incompatible with driver expectations. This paper proposes a robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework validated in the Vissim traffic simulator. The framework integrates three mechanisms: (1) Turning Ratio Randomization, a training strategy that exposes agents to dynamic turning probabilities to enhance robustness against unseen scenarios; (2) a stability-oriented Exponential Phase Duration Adjustment action space, which balances responsiveness and precision through cyclical, exponential phase adjustments; and (3) a Neighbor-Based Observation scheme utilizing the MAPPO algorithm with Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE). By leveraging centralized updates, this approach approximates the efficacy of global observations while maintaining scalable local communication. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework outperforms standard RL baselines, reducing average waiting time by over 10%. The proposed model exhibits superior generalization in unseen traffic scenarios and maintains high control stability, offering a practical solution for adaptive signal control.

AI.tldr: Proposes robust MARL framework for traffic signal control with three mechanisms: turning ratio randomization for generalization, exponential phase adjustment action space for stability, and neighbor-based observations with CTDE.
AI.motivation: RL in traffic signal control faces deployment challenges due to poor generalization to dynamic traffic flows and action spaces incompatible with driver expectations. Existing approaches overfit static patterns.
AI.method: Multi-Agent RL framework with three key mechanisms: 1) Turning Ratio Randomization during training to expose agents to dynamic scenarios, 2) Exponential Phase Duration Adjustment action space for stable control, 3) Neighbor-Based Observation scheme using MAPPO algorithm with Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution.
AI.result: Outperforms standard RL baselines, reducing average waiting time by over 10%. Exhibits superior generalization in unseen traffic scenarios and maintains high control stability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a practical solution for adaptive traffic signal control with improved robustness and generalization capabilities for real-world deployment.

title: Simple Recipe Works: Vision-Language-Action Models are Natural Continual Learners with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Continual Reinforcement Learning (CRL) for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is a promising direction toward self-improving embodied agents that can adapt in openended, evolving environments. However, conventional wisdom from continual learning suggests that naive Sequential Fine-Tuning (Seq. FT) leads to catastrophic forgetting, necessitating complex CRL strategies. In this work, we take a step back and conduct a systematic study of CRL for large pretrained VLAs across three models and five challenging lifelong RL benchmarks. We find that, contrary to established belief, simple Seq. FT with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is remarkably strong: it achieves high plasticity, exhibits little to no forgetting, and retains strong zero-shot generalization, frequently outperforming more sophisticated CRL methods. Through detailed analysis, we show that this robustness arises from a synergy between the large pretrained model, parameter-efficient adaptation, and on-policy RL. Together, these components reshape the stability-plasticity trade-off, making continual adaptation both stable and scalable. Our results position Sequential Fine-Tuning as a powerful method for continual RL with VLAs and provide new insights into lifelong learning in the large model era. Code is available at github.com/UT-Austin-RobIn/continual-vla-rl.

AI.tldr: Simple sequential fine-tuning with LoRA outperforms complex continual RL methods for vision-language-action models, showing minimal forgetting while maintaining strong generalization.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges conventional wisdom in continual learning that sequential fine-tuning leads to catastrophic forgetting, particularly for vision-language-action models in continual reinforcement learning settings.
AI.method: The authors conducted a systematic study across three models and five lifelong RL benchmarks, using sequential fine-tuning with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) as the primary method compared against more sophisticated continual RL strategies.
AI.result: Contrary to established belief, simple sequential fine-tuning with LoRA achieves high plasticity, exhibits little to no forgetting, retains strong zero-shot generalization, and frequently outperforms more sophisticated CRL methods.
AI.conclusion: Sequential fine-tuning with LoRA is a powerful method for continual RL with VLAs, reshaping the stability-plasticity trade-off through synergy between large pretrained models, parameter-efficient adaptation, and on-policy RL.

title: On Information Self-Locking in Reinforcement Learning for Active Reasoning of LLM agents
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) with outcome-based rewards has achieved significant success in training large language model (LLM) agents for complex reasoning tasks. However, in active reasoning where agents need to strategically ask questions to acquire task-relevant information, we find that LLM agents trained with RL often suffer from information self-locking: the agent ceases to ask informative questions and struggles to internalize already-obtained information. To understand the phenomenon, we decompose active reasoning into two core capabilities: Action Selection (AS), which determines the observation stream through queries, and Belief Tracking (BT), which updates the agent's belief based on collected evidence. We show that deficient AS and BT capabilities will limit the information exploration during RL training. Furthermore, insufficient exploration in turn hinders the improvement of AS and BT, creating a feedback loop that locks the agent in a low-information regime. To resolve the issue, we propose a simple yet effective approach that reallocates the learning signal by injecting easy- to-obtain directional critiques to help the agent escape self-locking. Extensive experiments with 7 datasets show that our approach significantly mitigates the information self-locking, bringing up to 60% improvements.

AI.tldr: A new RL approach that addresses "information self-locking" in LLM agents by reallocating learning signals with directional critiques, improving performance by up to 60% on active reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: RL-trained LLM agents suffer from "information self-locking" in active reasoning tasks where they need to ask strategic questions. The agents stop asking informative questions and fail to internalize obtained information, limiting their reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Decomposes active reasoning into Action Selection (determining queries) and Belief Tracking (updating beliefs). Proposes injecting easy-to-obtain directional critiques to reallocate learning signals, helping agents escape the self-locking feedback loop.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 7 datasets show the approach significantly mitigates information self-locking, achieving up to 60% improvements compared to baseline RL methods.
AI.conclusion: Information self-locking is a critical bottleneck in RL-trained LLM agents for active reasoning, and the proposed directional critique approach effectively addresses this issue by breaking the negative feedback loop between deficient exploration and capability improvement.

title: Context-dependent manifold learning: A neuromodulated constrained autoencoder approach
summary: Constrained autoencoders (cAE) provide a successful path towards interpretable dimensionality reduction by enforcing geometric structure on latent spaces. However, standard cAEs cannot adapt to varying physical parameters or environmental conditions without conflating these contextual shifts with the primary input. To address this, we integrated a neuromodulatory mechanism into the cAE framework to allow for context-dependent manifold learning. This paper introduces the Neuromodulated Constrained Autoencoder (NcAE), which adaptively parameterizes geometric constraints via gain and bias tuning conditioned on static contextual information. Experimental results on dynamical systems show that the NcAE accurately captures how manifold geometry varies across different regimes while maintaining rigorous projection properties. These results demonstrate that neuromodulation effectively decouples global contextual parameters from local manifold representations. This architecture provides a foundation for developing more flexible, physics-informed representations in systems subject to (non-stationary) environmental constraints.

AI.tldr: A neuromodulated constrained autoencoder (NcAE) that adapts geometric constraints based on contextual information to decouple global parameters from local manifold representations.
AI.motivation: Standard constrained autoencoders cannot adapt to varying physical parameters or environmental conditions without conflating these contextual shifts with the primary input, limiting their ability to capture context-dependent manifold geometry.
AI.method: Integrated a neuromodulatory mechanism into the constrained autoencoder framework, using gain and bias tuning conditioned on static contextual information to adaptively parameterize geometric constraints.
AI.result: The NcAE accurately captures how manifold geometry varies across different regimes while maintaining rigorous projection properties, effectively decoupling global contextual parameters from local manifold representations.
AI.conclusion: The neuromodulated architecture provides a foundation for developing more flexible, physics-informed representations in systems subject to non-stationary environmental constraints.

title: Increasing intelligence in AI agents can worsen collective outcomes
summary: When resources are scarce, will a population of AI agents coordinate in harmony, or descend into tribal chaos? Diverse decision-making AI from different developers is entering everyday devices -- from phones and medical devices to battlefield drones and cars -- and these AI agents typically compete for finite shared resources such as charging slots, relay bandwidth, and traffic priority. Yet their collective dynamics and hence risks to users and society are poorly understood. Here we study AI-agent populations as the first system of real agents in which four key variables governing collective behaviour can be independently toggled: nature (innate LLM diversity), nurture (individual reinforcement learning), culture (emergent tribe formation), and resource scarcity. We show empirically and mathematically that when resources are scarce, AI model diversity and reinforcement learning increase dangerous system overload, though tribe formation lessens this risk. Meanwhile, some individuals profit handsomely. When resources are abundant, the same ingredients drive overload to near zero, though tribe formation makes the overload slightly worse. The crossover is arithmetical: it is where opposing tribes that form spontaneously first fit inside the available capacity. More sophisticated AI-agent populations are not better: whether their sophistication helps or harms depends entirely on a single number -- the capacity-to-population ratio -- that is knowable before any AI-agent ships.

AI.tldr: AI agent populations with diverse LLMs and reinforcement learning can cause dangerous system overload under resource scarcity, though tribe formation mitigates this risk, while individual agents can profit. The outcome depends critically on the capacity-to-population ratio.
AI.motivation: As diverse AI agents from different developers increasingly compete for finite shared resources in everyday devices, there is poor understanding of their collective dynamics and the risks they pose to users and society.
AI.method: The study treats AI-agent populations as the first system where four key variables can be independently toggled: nature (innate LLM diversity), nurture (individual reinforcement learning), culture (emergent tribe formation), and resource scarcity. Empirical and mathematical analysis is conducted.
AI.result: When resources are scarce, AI model diversity and reinforcement learning increase dangerous system overload, though tribe formation lessens this risk. When resources are abundant, the same factors drive overload to near zero, though tribe formation slightly worsens overload. The crossover point is where opposing tribes first fit inside available capacity. More sophisticated AI populations are not inherently better; their impact depends on the capacity-to-population ratio.
AI.conclusion: The behavior of AI-agent populations is not determined by sophistication alone but by the capacity-to-population ratio, which can be known before deployment. This ratio is crucial for predicting whether diverse AI agents will coordinate harmoniously or descend into chaos when competing for shared resources.

title: Entropy-Preserving Reinforcement Learning
summary: Policy gradient algorithms have driven many recent advancements in language model reasoning. An appealing property is their ability to learn from exploration on their own trajectories, a process crucial for fostering diverse and creative solutions. As we show in this paper, many policy gradient algorithms naturally reduce the entropy -- and thus the diversity of explored trajectories -- as part of training, yielding a policy increasingly limited in its ability to explore. In this paper, we argue that entropy should be actively monitored and controlled throughout training. We formally analyze the contributions of leading policy gradient objectives on entropy dynamics, identify empirical factors (such as numerical precision) that significantly impact entropy behavior, and propose explicit mechanisms for entropy control. These include REPO, a family of algorithms that modify the advantage function to regulate entropy, and ADAPO, an adaptive asymmetric clipping approach. Models trained with our entropy-preserving methods maintain diversity throughout training, yielding final policies that are more performant and retain their trainability for sequential learning in new environments.

AI.tldr: Policy gradient algorithms often reduce entropy/diversity during training, limiting exploration. The paper proposes actively monitoring and controlling entropy using methods like REPO and ADAPO to preserve diversity and improve performance.
AI.motivation: Policy gradient algorithms naturally reduce entropy and diversity of explored trajectories during training, which limits the policy's ability to explore and generate creative solutions. This reduction in diversity hampers performance and trainability.
AI.method: Formally analyze entropy dynamics of policy gradient objectives, identify empirical factors affecting entropy behavior, and propose explicit entropy control mechanisms including REPO (algorithms modifying advantage function) and ADAPO (adaptive asymmetric clipping).
AI.result: Models trained with entropy-preserving methods maintain diversity throughout training, yielding final policies that are more performant and retain trainability for sequential learning in new environments.
AI.conclusion: Entropy should be actively monitored and controlled in policy gradient training to preserve exploration diversity, which leads to better-performing policies and improved sequential learning capabilities.

title: TopoBench: Benchmarking LLMs on Hard Topological Reasoning
comment: Accepted, Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models at ICLR 2026
summary: Solving topological grid puzzles requires reasoning over global spatial invariants such as connectivity, loop closure, and region symmetry and remains challenging for even the most powerful large language models (LLMs). To study these abilities under controlled settings, we introduce TopoBench, a benchmark of six puzzle families across three difficulty levels. We evaluate strong reasoning LLMs on TopoBench and find that even frontier models solve fewer than one quarter of hard instances, with two families nearly unsolved. To investigate whether these failures stem from reasoning limitations or from difficulty extracting and maintaining spatial constraints, we annotate 750 chain of thought traces with an error taxonomy that surfaces four candidate causal failure modes, then test them with targeted interventions simulating each error type. These interventions show that certain error patterns like premature commitment and constraint forgetting have a direct impact on the ability to solve the puzzle, while repeated reasoning is a benign effect of search. Finally we study mitigation strategies including prompt guidance, cell-aligned grid representations and tool-based constraint checking, finding that the bottleneck lies in extracting constraints from spatial representations and not in reasoning over them. Code and data are available at github.com/mayug/topobench-benchmark.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with topological grid puzzles due to difficulty extracting constraints from spatial representations, not reasoning over them. The TopoBench benchmark reveals specific failure modes like premature commitment and constraint forgetting, with mitigation strategies pointing to improved constraint extraction as key to better performance.
AI.motivation: Topological grid puzzles require reasoning over global spatial invariants like connectivity, loop closure, and region symmetry, which remain challenging for state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs). The authors aim to systematically study these reasoning abilities under controlled settings and identify specific failure modes and mitigation strategies.
AI.method: The authors created TopoBench, a benchmark with six puzzle families across three difficulty levels. They evaluated strong reasoning LLMs on this benchmark and collected 750 chain-of-thought traces. These traces were annotated with an error taxonomy to identify failure modes. Targeted interventions were then applied to simulate each error type and assess their impact. Additionally, they explored mitigation strategies such as prompt guidance, cell-aligned grid representations, and tool-based constraint checking to determine the primary bottlenecks.
AI.result: The authors introduced TopoBench, a benchmark of six puzzle families across three difficulty levels, and evaluated strong reasoning LLMs. They found that even frontier models solve fewer than one quarter of hard instances, with two families nearly unsolved. Through annotating 750 chain-of-thought traces with an error taxonomy, they identified four candidate causal failure modes and tested them with targeted interventions. The interventions showed that certain error patterns like premature commitment and constraint forgetting directly impact puzzle-solving ability, while repeated reasoning is a benign effect of search. They also studied mitigation strategies including prompt guidance, cell-aligned grid representations, and tool-based constraint checking, finding that the bottleneck lies in extracting constraints from spatial representations rather than reasoning over them.
AI.conclusion: The main bottleneck for LLMs in solving topological grid puzzles is not reasoning over constraints but extracting constraints from spatial representations. While certain error patterns like premature commitment and constraint forgetting significantly hinder performance, repeated reasoning is less detrimental. Mitigation strategies highlight the importance of improving constraint extraction from spatial data, suggesting that future work should focus on enhancing LLMs' ability to interpret and maintain spatial constraints effectively.

title: EvoFlows: Evolutionary Edit-Based Flow-Matching for Protein Engineering
comment: Accepted at Workshop on Foundation Models for Science: Real-World Impact and Science-First Design, ICLR 2026
summary: We introduce EvoFlows, a variable-length sequence-to-sequence protein modeling approach uniquely suited to protein engineering. Unlike autoregressive and masked language models, EvoFlows perform a limited, controllable number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions on a template protein sequence. In other words, EvoFlows predict not only _which_ mutation to perform, but also _where_ it should occur. Our approach leverages edit flows to learn mutational trajectories between evolutionarily-related protein sequences, simultaneously modeling distributions of related natural proteins and the mutational paths connecting them. Through extensive _in silico_ evaluation on diverse protein communities from UNIREF and OAS, we demonstrate that EvoFlows capture protein sequence distributions with a quality comparable to leading masked language models commonly used in protein engineering, while showing improved ability to generate non-trivial yet natural-like mutants from a given template protein.

AI.tldr: EvoFlows is a protein engineering approach that uses edit flows to model mutational trajectories between evolutionarily-related proteins, enabling controlled insertions, deletions, and substitutions at specific positions.
AI.motivation: Current protein modeling approaches (autoregressive and masked language models) don't explicitly model where mutations should occur in a protein sequence, limiting their utility for protein engineering where controlled, targeted modifications are needed.
AI.method: EvoFlows uses edit flows to learn mutational trajectories between evolutionarily-related protein sequences, modeling distributions of related natural proteins and the mutational paths connecting them. It performs a limited, controllable number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions on template sequences.
AI.result: EvoFlows captures protein sequence distributions with quality comparable to leading masked language models, while showing improved ability to generate non-trivial yet natural-like mutants from given template proteins, as demonstrated through extensive in silico evaluation on diverse protein communities from UNIREF and OAS.
AI.conclusion: EvoFlows provides a unique sequence-to-sequence protein modeling approach that enables controlled, targeted protein engineering by predicting both which mutations to perform and where they should occur, offering advantages over existing methods for generating natural-like protein variants.

title: Compiling Temporal Numeric Planning into Discrete PDDL+: Extended Version
comment: This paper is an extended version of the homonymous appearing in the ICAPS 2026 proceedings. This version provides the proofs and addidional explanations of the compilation
summary: Since the introduction of the PDDL+ modeling language, it was known that temporal planning with durative actions (as in PDDL 2.1) could be compiled into PDDL+. However, no practical compilation was presented in the literature ever since. We present a practical compilation from temporal planning with durative actions into PDDL+, fully capturing the semantics and only assuming the non-self-overlapping of actions. Our compilation is polynomial, retains the plan length up to a constant factor and is experimentally shown to be of practical relevance for hard temporal numeric problems.

AI.tldr: A practical polynomial compilation from temporal planning with durative actions into PDDL+, preserving semantics and plan length up to constant factor.
AI.motivation: Despite theoretical knowledge that temporal planning with durative actions could be compiled into PDDL+, no practical compilation existed in literature since PDDL+ was introduced.
AI.method: Develop a polynomial compilation from temporal planning with durative actions into PDDL+ that fully captures semantics, assuming only non-self-overlapping of actions.
AI.result: The compilation retains plan length up to a constant factor and is experimentally shown to be practically relevant for hard temporal numeric problems.
AI.conclusion: The work provides the first practical compilation from temporal planning with durative actions to PDDL+, making it usable for solving challenging temporal numeric problems.

title: Mitigating the Multiplicity Burden: The Role of Calibration in Reducing Predictive Multiplicity of Classifiers
summary: As machine learning models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, ensuring both probabilistic reliability and prediction stability has become critical. This paper examines the interplay between classification calibration and predictive multiplicity - the phenomenon in which multiple near-optimal models within the Rashomon set yield conflicting credit outcomes for the same applicant. Using nine diverse credit risk benchmark datasets, we investigate whether predictive multiplicity concentrates in regions of low predictive confidence and how post-hoc calibration can mitigate algorithmic arbitrariness. Our empirical analysis reveals that minority class observations bear a disproportionate multiplicity burden, as confirmed by significant disparities in predictive multiplicity and prediction confidence. Furthermore, our empirical comparisons indicate that applying post-hoc calibration methods - specifically Platt Scaling, Isotonic Regression, and Temperature Scaling - is associated with lower obscurity across the Rashomon set. Among the tested techniques, Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression provide the most robust reduction in predictive multiplicity. These findings suggest that calibration can function as a consensus-enforcing layer and may support procedural fairness by mitigating predictive multiplicity.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc calibration methods can reduce predictive multiplicity and algorithmic arbitrariness in credit risk models, with Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression showing the most robust effectiveness.
AI.motivation: As ML models are deployed in high-stakes environments like credit risk assessment, ensuring both probabilistic reliability and prediction stability is critical. The paper examines the interplay between classification calibration and predictive multiplicity (where multiple near-optimal models yield conflicting outcomes).
AI.method: The study uses nine diverse credit risk benchmark datasets to investigate predictive multiplicity concentration in low-confidence regions and how post-hoc calibration can mitigate algorithmic arbitrariness. Empirical analysis includes comparing post-hoc calibration methods (Platt Scaling, Isotonic Regression, Temperature Scaling) and examining their effects on predictive multiplicity.
AI.result: 1. Minority class observations bear disproportionate multiplicity burden, confirmed by significant disparities in predictive multiplicity and prediction confidence. 2. Applying post-hoc calibration methods is associated with lower obscurity across the Rashomon set. 3. Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression provide the most robust reduction in predictive multiplicity.
AI.conclusion: Calibration can function as a consensus-enforcing layer and may support procedural fairness by mitigating predictive multiplicity. The findings suggest practical approaches to reduce algorithmic arbitrariness in credit risk assessment systems.

title: Portfolio of Solving Strategies in CEGAR-based Object Packing and Scheduling for Sequential 3D Printing
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2503.05071
summary: Computing power that used to be available only in supercomputers decades ago especially their parallelism is currently available in standard personal computer CPUs even in CPUs for mobile telephones. We show how to effectively utilize the computing power of modern multi-core personal computer CPU to solve the complex combinatorial problem of object arrangement and scheduling for sequential 3D printing. We achieved this by parallelizing the existing CEGAR-SEQ algorithm that solves the sequential object arrangement and scheduling by expressing it as a linear arithmetic formula which is then solved by a technique inspired by counterexample guided abstraction refinement (CEGAR). The original CEGAR-SEQ algorithm uses an object arrangement strategy that places objects towards the center of the printing plate. We propose alternative object arrangement strategies such as placing objects towards a corner of the printing plate and scheduling objects according to their height. Our parallelization is done at the high-level where we execute the CEGAR-SEQ algorithm in parallel with a portfolio of object arrangement strategies, an algorithm is called Porfolio-CEGAR-SEQ. Our experimental evaluation indicates that Porfolio-CEGAR-SEQ outperforms the original CEGAR-SEQ. When a batch of objects for multiple printing plates is scheduled, Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ often uses fewer printing plates than CEGAR-SEQ.

AI.tldr: Parallelized version of CEGAR-SEQ algorithm using portfolio of object arrangement strategies improves 3D printing scheduling efficiency on multi-core CPUs.
AI.motivation: Modern multi-core CPUs in personal computers and mobile devices provide significant parallel computing power that can be leveraged to solve complex combinatorial problems like 3D printing object arrangement and scheduling more efficiently.
AI.method: Parallelized the existing CEGAR-SEQ algorithm by executing it concurrently with a portfolio of different object arrangement strategies (center placement, corner placement, height-based scheduling) on multi-core CPUs, creating Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ.
AI.result: Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ outperforms the original CEGAR-SEQ algorithm, often using fewer printing plates when scheduling batches of objects for multiple printing plates.
AI.conclusion: Effectively utilizing modern multi-core CPU parallelism through high-level parallelization with diverse arrangement strategies significantly improves 3D printing scheduling efficiency, demonstrating practical application of available computing power for complex combinatorial optimization.

title: Exploiting Expertise of Non-Expert and Diverse Agents in Social Bandit Learning: A Free Energy Approach
summary: Personalized AI-based services involve a population of individual reinforcement learning agents. However, most reinforcement learning algorithms focus on harnessing individual learning and fail to leverage the social learning capabilities commonly exhibited by humans and animals. Social learning integrates individual experience with observing others' behavior, presenting opportunities for improved learning outcomes. In this study, we focus on a social bandit learning scenario where a social agent observes other agents' actions without knowledge of their rewards. The agents independently pursue their own policy without explicit motivation to teach each other. We propose a free energy-based social bandit learning algorithm over the policy space, where the social agent evaluates others' expertise levels without resorting to any oracle or social norms. Accordingly, the social agent integrates its direct experiences in the environment and others' estimated policies. The theoretical convergence of our algorithm to the optimal policy is proven. Empirical evaluations validate the superiority of our social learning method over alternative approaches in various scenarios. Our algorithm strategically identifies the relevant agents, even in the presence of random or suboptimal agents, and skillfully exploits their behavioral information. In addition to societies including expert agents, in the presence of relevant but non-expert agents, our algorithm significantly enhances individual learning performance, where most related methods fail. Importantly, it also maintains logarithmic regret.

AI.tldr: Proposes a free energy–based social bandit algorithm that lets agents learn from others' actions (without reward info) by estimating expertise and integrating policies, achieving optimal convergence, outperforming alternatives, and maintaining logarithmic regret.
AI.motivation: Personalized AI services often rely on individual RL agents, but these algorithms typically ignore social learning—a key capability in humans and animals that combines individual experience with observing others' behavior to improve outcomes.
AI.method: A social bandit scenario where a social agent observes other agents' actions (without reward knowledge) while each agent independently pursues its own policy. The algorithm uses free energy over the policy space to estimate others' expertise levels without oracles or social norms, integrating direct experience with estimated policies of others.
AI.result: The proposed free energy–based social bandit learning algorithm converges theoretically to the optimal policy, outperforms alternative methods across scenarios, strategically identifies relevant agents (even among random/suboptimal ones), exploits their behavioral information, and maintains logarithmic regret.
AI.conclusion: The free energy–based social bandit learning algorithm enables effective social learning by estimating others' expertise and integrating their behavioral information, significantly boosting individual performance even with non-expert but relevant agents, while preserving theoretical guarantees like logarithmic regret.

title: Examining Reasoning LLMs-as-Judges in Non-Verifiable LLM Post-Training
summary: Reasoning LLMs-as-Judges, which can benefit from inference-time scaling, provide a promising path for extending the success of reasoning models to non-verifiable domains where the output correctness/quality cannot be directly checked. However, while reasoning judges have shown better performance on static evaluation benchmarks, their effectiveness in actual policy training has not been systematically examined. Therefore, we conduct a rigorous study to investigate the actual impact of non-reasoning and reasoning judges in reinforcement-learning-based LLM alignment. Our controlled synthetic setting, where a "gold-standard" judge (gpt-oss-120b) provides preference annotations to train smaller judges, reveals key differences between non-reasoning and reasoning judges: non-reasoning judges lead to reward hacking easily, while reasoning judges can lead to policies that achieve strong performance when evaluated by the gold-standard judge. Interestingly, we find that the reasoning-judge-trained policies achieve such strong performance by learning to generate highly effective adversarial outputs that can also score well on popular benchmarks such as Arena-Hard by deceiving other LLM-judges. Combined with our further analysis, our study highlights both important findings and room for improvements for applying (reasoning) LLM-judges in non-verifiable LLM post-training.

AI.tldr: Reasoning LLM judges outperform non-reasoning judges in RL-based LLM alignment by producing policies that generate effective adversarial outputs that score well on benchmarks, though they can deceive other LLM judges.
AI.motivation: While reasoning LLMs-as-Judges show promise for evaluating non-verifiable domains where output correctness cannot be directly checked, their actual effectiveness in policy training has not been systematically examined, creating a research gap.
AI.method: Conducted a rigorous study using a controlled synthetic setting where a gold-standard judge (GPT-o8s-120B) provides preference annotations to train smaller judges, comparing non-reasoning and reasoning judges in reinforcement-learning-based LLM alignment.
AI.result: Non-reasoning judges lead to reward hacking easily, while reasoning judges produce policies that achieve strong performance when evaluated by the gold-standard judge. However, these policies achieve success by generating highly effective adversarial outputs that can also deceive other LLM-judges on popular benchmarks like Arena-Hard.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals both important findings and room for improvement in applying reasoning LLM-judges for non-verifiable LLM post-training, highlighting that while reasoning judges outperform non-reasoning ones, they can produce policies that generate adversarial outputs that game evaluation systems.

title: A Further Efficient Algorithm with Best-of-Both-Worlds Guarantees for $m$-Set Semi-Bandit Problem
summary: This paper studies the optimality and complexity of Follow-the-Perturbed-Leader (FTPL) policy in $m$-set semi-bandit problems. FTPL has been studied extensively as a promising candidate of an efficient algorithm with favorable regret for adversarial combinatorial semi-bandits. Nevertheless, the optimality of FTPL has still been unknown unlike Follow-the-Regularized-Leader (FTRL) whose optimality has been proved for various tasks of online learning. In this paper, we extend the analysis of FTPL with geometric resampling (GR) to $m$-set semi-bandits, which is a special case of combinatorial semi-bandits, showing that FTPL with Fréchet and Pareto distributions with certain parameters achieves the best possible regret of $O(\sqrt{mdT})$ in adversarial setting. We also show that FTPL with Fréchet and Pareto distributions with a certain parameter achieves a logarithmic regret for stochastic setting, meaning the Best-of-Both-Worlds optimality of FTPL for $m$-set semi-bandit problems. Furthermore, we extend the conditional geometric resampling to $m$-set semi-bandits for efficient loss estimation in FTPL, reducing the computational complexity from $O(d^2)$ of the original geometric resampling to $O(md(\log(d/m)+1))$ without sacrificing the regret performance.

AI.tldr: FTPL achieves optimal regret bounds in m-set semi-bandits with Fréchet/Pareto distributions for adversarial and stochastic settings, plus computational improvements via conditional geometric resampling.
AI.motivation: FTPL has been studied as an efficient algorithm for adversarial combinatorial semi-bandits, but its optimality remained unknown unlike FTRL which has proven optimality. This paper aims to establish FTPL's optimality in m-set semi-bandit problems.
AI.method: Extend FTPL with geometric resampling (GR) to m-set semi-bandits, using Fréchet and Pareto distributions with specific parameters. Also develop conditional geometric resampling for efficient loss estimation.
AI.result: FTPL achieves O(√mdT) regret in adversarial setting (optimal) and logarithmic regret in stochastic setting (Best-of-Both-Worlds optimality). Computational complexity reduced from O(d²) to O(md(log(d/m)+1)) without performance loss.
AI.conclusion: FTPL with Fréchet/Pareto distributions achieves optimal regret bounds in m-set semi-bandits for both adversarial and stochastic settings, establishing its optimality. Conditional GR reduces computational cost without sacrificing regret performance.

title: Implicit Statistical Inference in Transformers: Approximating Likelihood-Ratio Tests In-Context
comment: Accepted at the Latent and Implicit Thinking Workshop (ICLR 2026)
summary: In-context learning (ICL) allows Transformers to adapt to novel tasks without weight updates, yet the underlying algorithms remain poorly understood. We adopt a statistical decision-theoretic perspective by investigating simple binary hypothesis testing, where the optimal policy is determined by the likelihood-ratio test. Notably, this setup provides a mathematically rigorous setting for mechanistic interpretability where the target algorithmic ground truth is known. By training Transformers on tasks requiring distinct geometries (linear shifted means vs. nonlinear variance estimation), we demonstrate that the models approximate the Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics from context up to some monotonic transformation, matching the performance of an ideal oracle estimator in nonlinear regimes. Leveraging this analytical ground truth, mechanistic analysis via logit lens and circuit alignment suggests that the model does not rely on a fixed kernel smoothing heuristic. Instead, it appears to adapt the point at which decisions become linearly decodable: exhibiting patterns consistent with a voting-style ensemble for linear tasks while utilizing a deeper sequential computation for nonlinear tasks. These findings suggest that ICL emerges from the construction of task-adaptive statistical estimators rather than simple similarity matching.

AI.tldr: Transformers performing in-context learning approximate Bayes-optimal statistics and adapt computational strategies based on task geometry, moving beyond simple similarity matching.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand the underlying algorithms of in-context learning (ICL) in Transformers, which currently remain poorly understood despite ICL's ability to adapt to novel tasks without weight updates.
AI.method: The authors adopt a statistical decision-theoretic perspective, focusing on simple binary hypothesis testing where the optimal policy is known (likelihood-ratio test). They train Transformers on tasks with distinct geometries (linear shifted means and nonlinear variance estimation). They then perform mechanistic analysis using the logit lens and circuit alignment to interpret the model's internal computations.
AI.result: Transformers trained on binary hypothesis testing tasks (linear shifted means vs. nonlinear variance estimation) approximate the Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics from context up to a monotonic transformation, matching oracle estimator performance in nonlinear regimes. Mechanistic analysis reveals the model does not use a fixed kernel smoothing heuristic but adapts the decision point where outputs become linearly decodable—showing voting-style ensemble patterns for linear tasks and deeper sequential computation for nonlinear tasks.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning in Transformers emerges from the construction of task-adaptive statistical estimators, not from simple similarity matching. The model adaptively chooses where decisions become linearly decodable, employing different computational strategies (voting-style vs. deeper sequential) depending on task linearity.

title: Language Generation with Replay: A Learning-Theoretic View of Model Collapse
summary: As scaling laws push the training of frontier large language models (LLMs) toward ever-growing data requirements, training pipelines are approaching a regime where much of the publicly available online text may be consumed. At the same time, widespread LLM usage increases the volume of machine-generated content on the web; together, these trends raise the likelihood of generated text re-entering future training corpora, increasing the associated risk of performance degradation often called model collapse. In practice, model developers address this concern through data cleaning, watermarking, synthetic-data policies, or, in some cases, blissful ignorance. However, the problem of model collapse in generative models has not been examined from a learning-theoretic perspective: we study it through the theoretical lens of the language generation in the limit framework, introducing a replay adversary that augments the example stream with the generator's own past outputs. Our main contribution is a fine-grained learning-theoretic characterization of when replay fundamentally limits generation: while replay is benign for the strongest notion of uniform generation, it provably creates separations for the weaker notions of non-uniform generation and generation in the limit. Interestingly, our positive results mirror heuristics widely used in practice, such as data cleaning, watermarking, and output filtering, while our separations show when these ideas can fail.

AI.tldr: Model collapse occurs when LLMs train on their own generated content, potentially degrading performance; the paper analyzes this theoretically using language generation frameworks and shows replay is benign for uniform generation but problematic for weaker generation notions.
AI.motivation: As LLMs train on increasingly large datasets, they risk consuming machine-generated content from previous model outputs, which can lead to performance degradation (model collapse). Current practical solutions are heuristic, lacking theoretical understanding.
AI.method: The authors use learning-theoretic analysis through the language generation in the limit framework, introducing a "replay adversary" that augments the example stream with the generator's own past outputs to study model collapse.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows replay is benign for the strongest notion of uniform generation but creates separations (provably problematic) for weaker notions of non-uniform generation and generation in the limit, explaining when practical heuristics can fail.
AI.conclusion: Model collapse has a nuanced theoretical foundation: while current practical solutions (data cleaning, watermarking) mirror positive theoretical results, the separations reveal fundamental limitations where these approaches may fail, providing theoretical justification for practical concerns.

title: Disentangled Representation Learning through Unsupervised Symmetry Group Discovery
summary: Symmetry-based disentangled representation learning leverages the group structure of environment transformations to uncover the latent factors of variation. Prior approaches to symmetry-based disentanglement have required strong prior knowledge of the symmetry group's structure, or restrictive assumptions about the subgroup properties. In this work, we remove these constraints by proposing a method whereby an embodied agent autonomously discovers the group structure of its action space through unsupervised interaction with the environment.
